userStories.title: Fix Query freshness defaults — remove staleTime: 0 overrides and opt-in refetchOnWindowFocus
userStories.overview:
Global query defaults set `staleTime: 5000` and `refetchOnWindowFocus: true` in `src/routes/__root.tsx#L19`. However, several high-traffic hooks override `staleTime` back to `0`:
- Tickets: `src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L326`
- Tags: `src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L449`
- Tag metadata: `src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L471`
Active Ralph sessions also poll every 5 seconds at `src/lib/hooks/ralph.ts#L99`.
This means focusing the window triggers broad refresh storms, and the query cache cannot hide latency effectively.
acceptanceCriteria: No hook uses `staleTime: 0` without explicit justification
`refetchOnWindowFocus` is off globally and opt-in per query
Window focus no longer triggers broad refetch storms
Data still refreshes appropriately after mutations via invalidation
userStories.description:
## Context

Global query defaults set `staleTime: 5000` and `refetchOnWindowFocus: true` in `src/routes/__root.tsx#L19`. However, several high-traffic hooks override `staleTime` back to `0`:

- Tickets: `src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L326`
- Tags: `src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L449`
- Tag metadata: `src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L471`

Active Ralph sessions also poll every 5 seconds at `src/lib/hooks/ralph.ts#L99`.

This means focusing the window triggers broad refresh storms, and the query cache cannot hide latency effectively.

## What to do

1. Remove `staleTime: 0` overrides from ticket and tag hooks. Use data-appropriate freshness windows (e.g., 30s for tickets, 60s for tags).
2. Change global `refetchOnWindowFocus` to `false`. Add it back as opt-in only on queries that truly need live freshness.
3. Keep polling only for actively viewed "live" surfaces (e.g., active Ralph session view).
4. Match `gcTime` to any long `staleTime` values per project memory guidelines.

## Key files

- `src/routes/__root.tsx`
- `src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts`
- `src/lib/hooks/ralph.ts`

## Acceptance criteria

- No hook uses `staleTime: 0` without explicit justification
- `refetchOnWindowFocus` is off globally and opt-in per query
- Window focus no longer triggers broad refetch storms
- Data still refreshes appropriately after mutations via invalidation

userStories.title: Memoize or split AppLayout provider value to reduce rerender fan-out
userStories.overview: `AppLayout` at `src/components/AppLayout.tsx#L160` reads 10+ hooks (projects, projectsWithAI, active sessions, settings, filters, sample data, modals, search, tags, docker, Ralph containers). The context value is rebuilt as a fresh object on every render at line 402 without `useMemo`. `AppContext.Provider` wraps the entire shell at line 587. This means any small state change in the shell causes all context consumers to rerender.
acceptanceCriteria: Context provider value is either memoized or split into narrower providers
Small UI state changes (e.g., toggling mobile menu) do not rerender unrelated consumers
No regressions in AppLayout functionality
`BoardHeader` no longer needs to re-subscribe to URL filter state (it already gets it from props)
userStories.description:
## Context

`AppLayout` at `src/components/AppLayout.tsx#L160` reads 10+ hooks (projects, projectsWithAI, active sessions, settings, filters, sample data, modals, search, tags, docker, Ralph containers). The context value is rebuilt as a fresh object on every render at line 402 without `useMemo`. `AppContext.Provider` wraps the entire shell at line 587. This means any small state change in the shell causes all context consumers to rerender.

## What to do

Either:
1. **Memoize** the provider value with `useMemo` (quick fix), or
2. **Split** the monolithic `AppContext` into smaller, focused contexts (filters, modals, search, shell UI) so consumers only subscribe to state they use.

Option 2 is more work but eliminates the coupling problem at the root.

Also consider:
- Moving route-specific fetching out of `AppLayout`
- Having leaf components read their own queries instead of receiving everything through context

## Key files

- `src/components/AppLayout.tsx`

## Acceptance criteria

- Context provider value is either memoized or split into narrower providers
- Small UI state changes (e.g., toggling mobile menu) do not rerender unrelated consumers
- No regressions in AppLayout functionality
- `BoardHeader` no longer needs to re-subscribe to URL filter state (it already gets it from props)

userStories.title: Narrow invalidation scope after mutations
userStories.overview: Mutations currently use broad invalidation patterns like `invalidateQueries({ queryKey: queryKeys.allTickets })` which refetches all ticket data even when only one ticket changed. This compounds with the aggressive freshness defaults to create unnecessary network and render work.
acceptanceCriteria: Mutations use narrow invalidation (specific keys, not broad parent keys)
Simple updates use `setQueryData` where possible
No stale data visible after mutations
Fewer network requests after common actions (status change, edit, comment)
userStories.description:
## Context

Mutations currently use broad invalidation patterns like `invalidateQueries({ queryKey: queryKeys.allTickets })` which refetches all ticket data even when only one ticket changed. This compounds with the aggressive freshness defaults to create unnecessary network and render work.

## What to do

1. After ticket mutations, invalidate only the specific ticket's query key, not all tickets.
2. Use direct cache writes (`setQueryData`) for simple updates where the new value is known.
3. Invalidate parent lists only when the change affects list-level display (e.g., status change on board).

## Key files

- `src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts` (mutation hooks)
- `src/lib/hooks/projects.ts` (mutation hooks)
- Other files with `invalidateQueries` calls

## Acceptance criteria

- Mutations use narrow invalidation (specific keys, not broad parent keys)
- Simple updates use `setQueryData` where possible
- No stale data visible after mutations
- Fewer network requests after common actions (status change, edit, comment)

techStack: Database: SQLite with better-sqlite3, Drizzle ORM
State: TanStack Query for server state

dos: Use Drizzle ORM: `db.select().from(tickets)`
Use typed schema imports: `import { tickets } from "../lib/schema"`
Use `eq()`, `and()`, `sql` from drizzle-orm for conditions
Use transactions for multi-table operations: `db.transaction(() => {...})`
Use `.get()` for single row, `.all()` for multiple
donts: Raw SQL strings: `db.run("SELECT * FROM tickets")`
String-based table names
String concatenation for WHERE clauses
Multiple independent queries that should be atomic
Assume query returns what you expect without checking

dos: Use `useQuery` with `queryKeys` for data fetching
Use `useMutation` with `onSuccess` for state changes
Invalidate queries after mutations: `queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: queryKeys.tickets })`
Use centralized `queryKeys` object from `src/lib/hooks.ts`
Create custom hooks in `src/lib/hooks.ts` for reusable query logic
donts: `useState` + `useEffect` for fetched data
Direct API calls without proper cache invalidation
Manual cache manipulation or refetch after every change
Hardcoded query key strings throughout components
Duplicate query setup in multiple components

dos: Use Tailwind classes for static styles: `className="p-4 bg-slate-800"`
Use CSS variables for theming: `var(--bg-primary)`, `var(--shadow-modal)`
Reserve inline styles for dynamic/computed values: `style={{ width: `${percent}%` }}`
Define shared style constants at module level for referential stability
Use `React.CSSProperties` type for inline style objects when needed
donts: Inline style objects for single-use styles
Hardcoded colors that don't adapt to themes
Mix Tailwind and inline styles inconsistently
Create new objects inside components that are reused
Untyped style objects that miss IDE autocomplete

dos: Use `createServerFn({ method: "GET" })` for reads, `"POST"` for writes
Use `.inputValidator()` for type-safe input handling
Return typed data directly: `return db.select().from(tickets).all()`
Use `ensureExists()` for required lookups: `ensureExists(project, "Project")`
Import from `@tanstack/react-start` (not `@tanstack/react-start/server`)
donts: Mix GET/POST inconsistently
Access raw input without validation
Wrap in unnecessary response objects
Return null and let caller handle missing data
Wrong import path

dos: Use Zod schemas for input validation: `{ ticketId: z.string() }`
Return structured `{ content: [{ type: "text", text: ... }] }` format
Set `isError: true` for error responses
Use `log.info()` / `log.error()` from `../lib/logging.js`
Include helpful error messages: `"Project not found. Use list_projects to see available."`
donts: Trust input without validation
Return plain strings
Return error text without the error flag
`console.log` in MCP server code
Generic "Not found" errors

dos: Test user behavior: what users see, click, and experience
Integration tests for workflows: test components together
Real database fixtures with actual schema
Tests that fail when user-facing behavior breaks
Ask: "Does this test catch bugs users would encounter?"
donts: Test implementation details, internal state, or private methods
Unit tests for every function
Excessive mocking of internals
Tests that break on refactoring internals
Chase 100% code coverage as a goal

Run `pnpm lint` - must pass with no errors
Run `pnpm test` - all tests must pass
Added tests for new functionality (ONLY tests that verify real user behavior - see Testing Philosophy above)
Used typed error classes (not generic `Error`)
Used Drizzle ORM (not raw SQL) - see DO/DON'T table above
Followed existing patterns from DO/DON'T tables
No hardcoded values that should be configurable
Existing tests still pass
No regressions in related functionality
Updated tests if behavior changed
Did not break backward compatibility (unless explicitly requested)
Manually verified in browser at `localhost:4242`
Checked responsive layout
Verified TanStack Query invalidates and updates correctly
Accessibility: keyboard navigation works, proper ARIA labels
Migration file created via `pnpm db:generate`
Migration tested with `pnpm db:migrate`
Backup tested if schema changed (use `pnpm brain-dump backup` then test restore)
Updated `src/lib/schema.ts` with proper types and constraints
Tested tool via Claude Code integration
Verified error responses are informative (see DO/DON'T table)
Updated tool documentation if interface changed
Added Zod schema for input validation
All acceptance criteria from ticket met
Work summary added via `comment` tool, `action: "add"` (for Ralph sessions)
Session completed with appropriate outcome (for Ralph sessions)
Committed with proper message format: `feat(<ticket-id>): <description>`

# Frontend Performance Report

Date: 2026-03-15

## Executive summary

The app is already using TanStack Router, Query, and Form correctly in a few places, but the current frontend still has three clear performance ceilings:

1. Too much JavaScript is loaded up front.
2. Too many views subscribe to broad, always-fresh data.
3. A few large screens do expensive work in render paths that should be pushed down, deferred, or lazy loaded.

The biggest wins are not micro-optimizations. They are:

- Cut initial bundle weight with route and component lazy loading.
- Reduce broad query invalidation and `staleTime: 0` usage.
- Stop loading full ticket/tag datasets just to compute counts or inactive views.
- Break up `AppLayout` and `TicketModal` so small state changes do not fan out through the app shell.

## What I measured

Production build output from `pnpm build`:

- Client `main` chunk: `770.18 kB` minified, `223.86 kB` gzip
- Client dashboard chunk: `458.67 kB` minified, `120.92 kB` gzip
- Client ticket detail chunk: `90.64 kB` minified, `19.70 kB` gzip
- Client board chunk: `72.67 kB` minified, `23.82 kB` gzip

The build also emitted Vite's large chunk warning. That is the clearest signal that perceived slowness is not just runtime rendering. The app is shipping a lot of JS before the user can feel it "waiting on them".

## Scope and limitations

This report is primarily based on:

- static code analysis
- query/state flow inspection
- production build output

It does not yet include:

- React Profiler traces
- Lighthouse or Web Vitals measurements
- network waterfall captures
- long-session memory/leak profiling
- SSR hydration timing analysis beyond static inspection

So the findings here are strong enough to prioritize work, but they should be followed by runtime profiling to validate the biggest wins and catch interaction bottlenecks that static analysis cannot prove.

## Findings

### 0. State management is workable, but not close to "most effective"

Current state strategy:

- TanStack Query for server state
- TanStack Form in some newer surfaces
- URL state for filters
- React local state for most UI concerns
- A broad app-shell context for cross-route coordination

That is a reasonable stack, but the implementation is uneven. The main issues are:

- shell state and route state are coupled too tightly
- some state is duplicated in multiple places
- heavy feature components still own too many concerns at once

Evidence:

- `AppLayout` acts as a global coordinator for filters, modals, refresh state, search routing, mobile shell state, project data, settings, and Ralph state in src/components/AppLayout.tsx#L160.
- The provider value is a fresh object every render in src/components/AppLayout.tsx#L402.
- Search behavior exists in both src/components/navigation/SearchBar.tsx#L55 and src/components/AppLayout.tsx#L705.
- `BoardHeader` re-subscribes to URL filter state even though the route already derived and passed filters in src/components/board/BoardHeader.tsx#L39.
- `TicketModal` still owns a very large mixed bag of state even after moving to TanStack Form in src/components/TicketModal.tsx#L183.

Assessment:

- Server state: mostly fine, but too eager and too broad.
- UI state: functional, but spread across large components.
- Cross-route state: too centralized in the shell.
- Form state: improving, but not consistently isolated at field/section level.

Best options:

- Split shell context into smaller providers or route-local hooks.
- Standardize one search implementation.
- Stop double-subscribing to URL/router state where props already exist.
- Push bulky modal and detail-screen state down into section components.

### 1. Initial JS cost is too high

Evidence:

- Devtools are mounted unconditionally in the root shell in src/routes/__root.tsx#L92.
- Router preload is effectively disabled with `defaultPreloadStaleTime: 0` in src/router.tsx#L13.
- Dashboard eagerly renders eight chart cards in src/components/dashboard/AnalyticsSection.tsx#L59.
- Multiple dashboard cards pull in `recharts`, for example src/components/dashboard/AIUsageChart.tsx#L3.

Impact:

- Slower initial parse and hydration.
- Higher input delay on lower-end hardware.
- Dashboard becomes a "big bang" route instead of progressive UI.

Best options:

- Gate devtools to development only.
- Convert deep routes to lazy routes and split heavy leaf components with `React.lazy` or TanStack Router lazy routes.
- Lazy load dashboard charts and render them on demand.
- Lazy load heavyweight modals and panels that are not visible on first paint: `TicketModal`, settings, feedback, import, git history, telemetry.
- Turn route preloading back on for common paths like board, list, and ticket detail.

### 2. Query freshness defaults are too aggressive

Evidence:

- Global query defaults already use a non-zero `staleTime` of 5 seconds in src/routes/__root.tsx#L19, but several high-traffic hooks override that back to `0`.
- Global query defaults also use `refetchOnWindowFocus: true` in src/routes/__root.tsx#L19.
- Tickets are always stale in src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L326.
- Tags are always stale in src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L449.
- Tag metadata is always stale in src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L471.
- Active Ralph sessions poll every 5 seconds by default in src/lib/hooks/ralph.ts#L99.

Impact:

- Focusing the window can trigger broad refresh storms.
- Query cache has less opportunity to hide latency.
- Background polling competes with user-triggered work.

Best options:

- Make `refetchOnWindowFocus` opt-in for the few queries that truly need it.
- Replace blanket `staleTime: 0` with data-specific freshness windows.
- Keep live polling only for actively viewed "live" surfaces.
- Use manual invalidation or event-driven refresh for MCP-driven updates instead of assuming every collection is stale all the time.

### 3. The app shell pulls in too much global data

Evidence:

- `AppLayout` reads projects, project-with-AI data, active sessions, settings, filters, sample data, modals, and more in one component in src/components/AppLayout.tsx#L160.
- The context value is rebuilt as a fresh object on every render in src/components/AppLayout.tsx#L402.
- `AppContext.Provider` wraps the whole shell in src/components/AppLayout.tsx#L587.

Impact:

- Small local updates can cause large rerender fan-out.
- Header, sidebar, route content, and modal state stay more tightly coupled than necessary.

Best options:

- Split app state into narrower contexts: filters, modal control, search navigation, shell UI.
- Memoize provider values.
- Move route-specific fetching out of `AppLayout` where possible.
- Prefer colocated query reads in leaf components over one huge shell coordinator.

### 4. Some aggregate views fetch far more data than they need

Evidence:

- `useProjectsWithAIActivity` loads all tickets just to derive per-project counts in src/lib/hooks/projects.ts#L133.
- The project detail page loads all project tickets just to count tickets per epic in src/routes/projects.$projectId.tsx#L21.
- `useProjects` does a project list call plus one epic query per project in src/lib/hooks/projects.ts#L92.

Impact:

- Extra query work even on screens that only need counts or summaries.
- More cache churn and more CPU spent on derived aggregation.

Best options:

- Add lightweight summary endpoints:
  - project counts
  - epic ticket counts
  - projects with epics in one server call
- Use `select` in Query for derived shapes when you already have the raw data.
- Stop using full ticket lists as a count API.

### 5. Some routes fetch data even when the view is inactive

Evidence:

- The list route always calls `useTagsWithMetadata(tagFilters)` in src/routes/list.tsx#L97, while `tagFilters` is a stable empty object and `useTagsWithMetadata` has no `enabled` guard in src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L471. That means the query runs regardless of whether the user is in ticket or tag sub-mode.
- `BoardHeader` subscribes to URL filters again via `useFiltersWithUrl()` even though the route already passes filter props in src/components/board/BoardHeader.tsx#L39.

Impact:

- Unnecessary background work on the list route.
- Extra router subscriptions and avoidable header rerenders.

Best options:

- Gate tag metadata query with `enabled: listSubMode === "tags"`.
- Let `BoardHeader` receive the already-derived filter callbacks instead of re-subscribing to router state.

### 6. Ticket detail is doing too much in one component

Evidence:

- `TicketModal` is a very large component that owns form state, polling, comments, attachments, launch actions, services, Ralph status, and delete flow in src/components/TicketModal.tsx#L183.
- The form validates on every change in src/components/TicketModal.tsx#L205.
- The same component also subscribes to comments polling, services polling, active Ralph session polling, and tag fetching in src/components/TicketModal.tsx#L276.

Impact:

- Editing a field can rerun work unrelated to the edited field.
- Ticket detail becomes harder to keep responsive as features accumulate.

Best options:

- Split ticket detail into lazy sections:
  - core metadata
  - activity/comments
  - telemetry
  - demo/review panels
  - attachments/services
- Move form validation from `onChange` to `onBlur` or submit for heavier schemas.
- Keep only the fields that truly need live subscriptions connected to the form store.

### 7. List and comment rendering still do avoidable per-row work

Evidence:

- `TicketListView` parses JSON for tags and subtasks inside row rendering in src/components/TicketListView.tsx#L150.
- `Comment` reparses markdown and recomputes relative timestamps during render in src/components/tickets/Comment.tsx#L48 and src/components/tickets/Comment.tsx#L143.

Impact:

- The cost scales with row count and comment length.
- This is not a crisis at low volume, but it becomes noticeable as tickets/comments grow.

Best options:

- Normalize parsed ticket fields earlier, ideally in the server layer or query `select`.
- Precompute comment render models or memoize rendered markdown by `comment.id + updatedAt`.
- Consider virtualization for long ticket tables, comment timelines, tags, and git history once datasets grow further.

### 8. Type safety is not end-to-end yet

The app is typed, but not fully type-safe across the boundary from DB/API to hooks to UI. There are still several places where runtime shape is trusted and then cast, or where domain types are duplicated instead of shared.

Evidence:

- `lib/schema.ts` already exports inferred DB types like `Ticket` and `TicketComment` in src/lib/schema.ts#L151.
- `useTickets` defines a separate handwritten `Ticket` interface in src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L32.
- Hook query functions cast server results back into expected shapes, for example:
  - comments in src/lib/hooks/comments.ts#L28
  - claude tasks in src/lib/hooks/claude-tasks.ts#L29
  - epic detail in src/lib/hooks/projects.ts#L519
  - tickets in src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L321
- Comment storage is broad at the schema/API boundary:
  - DB schema stores `author` and `type` as text in src/lib/schema.ts#L75
  - API `Comment` type exposes `author: string` and `type: string` in src/api/comments.ts#L24
- Route search state is manually typed with `Record<string, string>` instead of validated route search schemas in src/routes/list.tsx#L39.
- Route params and status values are frequently narrowed with casts instead of validated unions, for example `ticket.status as TicketStatus` patterns throughout the ticket and board surfaces.

Impact:

- Hidden type drift between schema, API DTOs, and UI models.
- More `JSON.parse` and more defensive fallback logic in components.
- Bugs can survive compilation because casts reassert correctness instead of proving it.

Best options:

- Pick one canonical domain layer for app-facing models.
  - Best option here: infer from schema or validated server DTOs, then reuse everywhere.
- Remove handwritten duplicate `Ticket` and `Comment` models where possible.
- Return strongly typed DTOs from server functions without re-casting in hooks.
- Add route `validateSearch` schemas so URL state is typed at the router boundary.
- Narrow string fields at the schema/API edge, not inside components.
- Normalize JSON-backed fields before they reach render code.

Bottom line:

- The app is not "unsafe", but it is not yet the kind of type-safe system where the compiler is doing maximum work for you.
- Right now the codebase still leans on "fetch, then cast, then defensively parse".
- The better target is "validate/normalize once at the boundary, then trust the type all the way up".

### 9. Server-side and data-layer work is part of the performance story

Several of the frontend bottlenecks are symptoms of server/data-shape issues rather than purely client rendering problems.

Evidence:

- `useProjectsWithAIActivity` currently derives counts by loading full ticket collections in src/lib/hooks/projects.ts#L133.
- `useProjects` triggers one epic fetch per project in src/lib/hooks/projects.ts#L92.
- Ticket list endpoints return full ticket objects, including JSON-backed text fields, from src/api/tickets.ts#L86.

Impact:

- Some of the best frontend wins require new summary endpoints, field-selected queries, or better SQL/index design.
- If those are not addressed, the client will keep doing too much work with too much data.

Best options:

- Add summary endpoints for project/epic counts.
- Add ticket-summary queries for board/list/project views.
- Add composite indexes for common filters.
- Consolidate analytics and epic-detail query plans on the server.

### 10. Long-lived polling and subscriptions should be profiled, not assumed safe

Evidence:

- Active Ralph sessions poll every 5 seconds in src/lib/hooks/ralph.ts#L99.
- Ticket detail combines multiple live subscriptions in src/components/TicketModal.tsx#L276.
- `useTickets` tracks previous tickets in a ref for status diffing in src/lib/hooks/tickets.ts#L315.

Impact:

- These are not obviously broken, but they should be verified under long-running sessions for timer buildup, subscription duplication, and retained data growth.

Best options:

- Add a profiling pass for 30-60 minute active sessions.
- Confirm polling is disabled when screens are hidden or not mounted.
- Check for unnecessary ref retention and repeated observer/timer setup.

### 11. Splash screen is intentionally delaying access to the app

Evidence:

- The root shell always renders `SplashScreen` first in src/routes/__root.tsx#L87.
- `SplashScreen` enforces a minimum display time of `800ms` plus an `800ms` fade duration in src/components/SplashScreen.tsx#L18.
- That means the overlay can block the app for roughly `1.6s` before disappearing in src/components/SplashScreen.tsx#L57.

Impact:

- This may dominate perceived performance on fast local loads.
- It can mask some real wins or losses in route/data performance because the user is gated by animation first.

Best options:

- Decide whether the splash is a branding choice or a loading strategy.
- If it is branding, reduce the minimum display time sharply or show it only on cold app boot.
- If it is meant to hide loading, replace it with route-aware pending states and faster progressive rendering.

### 12. SSR is present, but SSR/hydration performance has not been validated

Evidence:

- The app is built on TanStack Start with SSR-capable routing and server functions.
- The generated route tree indicates SSR is enabled in src/routeTree.gen.ts#L195.
- The root document is structured as an SSR shell in src/routes/__root.tsx#L67.

Impact:

- SSR may already be helping first paint, but this report does not yet measure how much.
- Hydration cost may still be large because the app shell and initial JS payload are heavy.
- If the splash screen blocks everything, some SSR benefit may be hidden from the user.

Best options:

- Measure server-rendered first paint vs hydrated interactivity.
- Check for hydration mismatches or unnecessary client-only rework.
- Prefer SSR + route loader data + progressive hydration over blocking overlays.

### 13. Static asset strategy was not deeply audited

This report does not yet cover:

- image optimization
- font loading strategy
- static asset cache headers

That is a secondary concern here because the largest current issues are app-shell JS, query behavior, and route/data architecture, but it should still be reviewed before calling the frontend audit "complete".

## Prioritized action list

### Quick wins

These should give the best return for low implementation risk.

1. Disable devtools in production.
2. Change the list route so tag metadata is only fetched in tag view.
3. Stop global `refetchOnWindowFocus` and add it only where needed.
4. Give tickets and tags a non-zero `staleTime` unless the screen is explicitly live.
5. Re-enable Router preload for high-traffic routes.
6. Add `validateSearch` to routes that read URL search state.
7. Consolidate duplicated search UI onto one component and one state path.
8. Memoize or split `AppLayout` provider state.
9. Memoize `SortableTicketCard` and stabilize hot-path board props.
10. Replace `ProjectsPanel` hover state with CSS-only hover behavior.

### Medium effort, high payoff

1. Replace full-ticket count derivations with summary APIs.
2. Collapse `useProjects` N+1 fetching into a single server response.
3. Split `TicketModal` into smaller lazy sections.
4. Move heavier form validation off `onChange`.
5. Normalize ticket row data before render so list rows do not `JSON.parse` repeatedly.
6. Replace duplicated handwritten domain interfaces with shared DTO/schema-derived types.
7. Remove hook-level result casts by typing server function outputs more precisely.
8. Add route loaders with `ensureQueryData`, after wiring `queryClient` into router context.
9. Add `preload="intent"` on likely next-click links after loaders exist.
10. Standardize missing query keys and stop bypassing the query key factory.
11. Narrow invalidation scope and add optimistic updates for status/position mutations.
12. Lazy load dashboard charts and inactive ticket-detail sections.

### Bigger bets

1. Add route loaders with `ensureQueryData` so route transitions arrive pre-warmed.
2. Add virtualization for long collections using TanStack Virtual.
3. Push live updates toward event-driven invalidation instead of repeated polling.
4. Consider replacing some dashboard charts with lighter-weight SVG or server-rendered summaries if the charts are not interacted with often.
5. Add composite indexes for the most common filter/query paths.
6. Move analytics and epic-detail aggregation deeper into SQL or batched query plans.
7. Add field-selected ticket summary endpoints for board/list/project pages.
8. Paginate large result sets where lists can grow without bound.

## TanStack-specific recommendations

### TanStack Router

- Use lazy routes for board, ticket detail, epic detail, dashboard subtrees.
- Add `queryClient` to router context before adopting loader-driven prefetch.
- Use loaders plus `ensureQueryData` for:
  - board ticket lists
  - ticket detail
  - epic detail
  - project detail
- Turn on intent-based preloading for likely next clicks.
- Add pending components so navigation feels instant even when data is still in flight.

### TanStack Query

- Centralize `queryOptions` definitions so freshness and invalidation rules are explicit.
- Use `select` for derived data like counts, grouped maps, and normalized ticket row models.
- Replace broad `invalidateQueries({ queryKey: queryKeys.allTickets })` where possible with narrower keys or direct cache writes.
- Keep polling localized to surfaces the user is actively looking at.

### TanStack Form

- Keep `useStore` selectors narrow like you already do, but move more of the UI into field-level subscribers.
- Validate expensive schemas on blur or submit, not every keystroke.
- Break giant forms into section components so unrelated fields do not rerender together.

## Recommended implementation order

If the goal is for the app to feel noticeably faster quickly, I would do this in order:

1. Production devtools off, route preloading on, list tag query gating.
2. Fix Query freshness defaults and reduce stale-everything behavior.
3. Add route search validation and consolidate duplicated search state/UI.
4. Split `AppLayout` so shell state changes stop rippling through the app.
5. Wire `queryClient` into router context, then add route loaders and `preload="intent"`.
6. Lazy load dashboard charts and heavy ticket-detail sections.
7. Replace count-by-fetching-full-lists with summary endpoints.
8. Fix N+1 and heavy query waterfalls in projects, epic detail, and analytics.
9. Unify domain types and remove cast-heavy hook boundaries.
10. Add targeted optimistic updates, narrower invalidation, and standardized query keys.
11. Add virtualization only after the above if large datasets still feel heavy.

## Merged execution plan

This is the version I would actually run in the repo after comparing both audits.

### Sprint 1: Shell and navigation feel

Goal: make normal navigation stop feeling heavier than it should.

1. Disable devtools in production.
2. Stop always fetching tag metadata in ticket-list mode.
3. Move Query freshness defaults away from "everything is stale".
4. Add `validateSearch` where routes read URL state.
5. Consolidate duplicated search behavior into one shared path.
6. Re-enable preload strategy for likely next routes.
7. Memoize or split the `AppLayout` provider value.

### Sprint 2: Preload data before the user asks for it

Goal: route transitions should already be warm.

1. Add `queryClient` to router context.
2. Add route loaders using `ensureQueryData`.
3. Add `preload="intent"` on high-frequency links.
4. Add pending components/skeletons tuned for route transitions.

### Sprint 3: Reduce unnecessary work per screen

Goal: stop paying full-list/full-view costs for summary screens.

1. Replace project and epic count derivations with summary endpoints.
2. Collapse N+1 project/epic loading into a single server response.
3. Batch or consolidate epic-detail and analytics query waterfalls.
4. Add ticket-summary endpoints or field-selected queries for board/list/project views.
5. Add missing composite indexes for common filters.

### Sprint 4: Break up heavy UI surfaces

Goal: the big screens should load and rerender in pieces, not as monoliths.

1. Split `TicketModal` into lazy sections or tabs.
2. Lazy load dashboard chart components.
3. Normalize ticket row data before render.
4. Memoize markdown/comment render work where it is reused.
5. Replace hover-driven state churn in `ProjectsPanel` with CSS.
6. Memoize `SortableTicketCard` and stabilize hot-loop callbacks.

### Sprint 5: Tighten correctness and perceived speed

Goal: make the app both safer and more responsive under mutation-heavy flows.

1. Remove handwritten duplicate domain types where schema or DTO types already exist.
2. Remove hook-level `as` casts by returning strongly typed DTOs from server functions.
3. Standardize all query keys through the query key factory.
4. Narrow invalidation scope after mutations.
5. Add optimistic updates for status and position changes.
6. Move form validation to blur/submit where live validation is not necessary.

### Sprint 6: Scale-oriented work

Goal: keep performance stable as project size grows.

1. Add virtualization to long ticket, tag, comment, and git-history lists.
2. Paginate result sets that can grow unbounded.
3. Shift more analytics aggregation into SQL or pre-aggregated shapes.
4. Replace polling with event-driven invalidation where practical.

### Sprint 7: Runtime validation and measurement

Goal: confirm the static audit with real traces and lock in measurable outcomes.

1. Capture React Profiler traces for:
   - board navigation
   - ticket detail open/edit/close
   - dashboard initial load
2. Record network waterfalls for:
   - app boot
   - board route navigation
   - ticket route navigation
   - dashboard route navigation
3. Measure long-session behavior with polling-heavy screens open.
4. Measure SSR-to-hydration timing and look for hydration mismatch work.
5. Review the splash screen's effect on perceived load timing.
6. Review image/font/static-asset delivery and caching.

## Suggested success metrics

These are reasonable targets for the first implementation pass.

### Build and bundle targets

- Reduce client `main` chunk below `500 kB` minified.
- Reduce dashboard route chunk by at least `30-40%`.
- Remove production devtools from shipped JS.

### Data-loading targets

- Eliminate avoidable inactive-view queries on list/board shell surfaces.
- Reduce duplicate mount/refocus ticket fetches during board -> ticket -> board flows.
- Replace N+1 project/epic loading with a single projects-with-epics request.

### UX targets

- Board/list/ticket route transitions should show data-prefetch behavior rather than waiting for post-mount fetch.
- Ticket modal open time should improve noticeably after section splitting and lazy loading.
- Dragging and status changes should feel immediate after optimistic updates and reduced rerender fan-out.
- Cold boot should not be dominated by a blocking splash animation unless that is an intentional product choice.

### Runtime profiling targets

- Establish baseline and post-fix traces for:
  - initial route render duration
  - splash-to-interactive duration
  - ticket modal commit duration
  - dashboard interaction latency
  - SSR first paint vs hydrated interactivity gap
  - polling-heavy long-session memory behavior

## Expected payoff

If you do only the first three steps, the app should feel meaningfully snappier:

- faster first interaction
- fewer focus-triggered refetch spikes
- smoother board/list/dashboard navigation
- less "whole app shivers" when small UI state changes

If you also do the summary endpoints and shell split, the app should start feeling much more intentional under load instead of "constantly recomputing everything just in case".
